subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Priya says: "The Marradong fun run raised more money than any other community event in our town last year. It also brought hundreds of visitors to local shops, and the volunteers say they enjoyed organising it. The council should therefore make the fun run an annual event."

Which one of the following best expresses the main conclusion of Priya's argument?
option_a: The fun run raised more money than any other community event in the town last year.
option_b: The council should make the fun run an annual event.
option_c: The volunteers enjoyed organising the fun run.
option_d: Community events always bring visitors to local shops.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"This is one of the reasons Priya gives, not the claim her reasons are meant to support.","B":"Correct: The word \"therefore\" signals this as the main conclusion; every other statement is offered in support of it.","C":"This is a supporting reason, not the conclusion of the argument.","D":"Priya never makes this general claim; she only says this fun run brought visitors to local shops."}
worked_solution: The conclusion of an argument is the claim that the other statements are trying to support. Priya gives three reasons - the money raised, the visitors to shops, and the volunteers' enjoyment - and the word "therefore" introduces the claim they support: the council should make the fun run annual. Options A and C are reasons, and D is a general claim Priya never makes. The correct answer is therefore B.
short_explanation: "Therefore" signals the conclusion: the council should make the fun run annual. The other statements are reasons or claims never made.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
A local newspaper editorial states: "Shade from street trees can lower footpath temperatures by several degrees on a hot day. Our town should therefore plant more trees along its main shopping streets. Summer heatwaves are becoming more frequent, and shoppers stay away from streets that offer no relief from the sun."

Which one of the following best expresses the main conclusion of the editorial?
option_a: The town should plant more trees along its main shopping streets.
option_b: Shade from street trees can lower footpath temperatures by several degrees.
option_c: Summer heatwaves are becoming more frequent.
option_d: Shoppers avoid the town's main shopping streets all year round.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Correct: This is the recommendation that all three other statements are given to support, even though it appears in the middle of the passage.","B":"This is a reason offered in support of planting more trees, not the conclusion.","C":"This is a supporting reason explaining why shade matters, not the conclusion.","D":"The editorial says shoppers avoid streets with no relief from the sun; it does not claim they avoid the main streets all year round."}
worked_solution: A conclusion does not have to come last; here it sits in the middle of the passage, marked by "therefore". The facts about cooler footpaths, more frequent heatwaves and shopper behaviour are all reasons for the recommendation to plant more trees. Option D twists the final sentence into a much stronger claim the editorial never makes. The correct answer is therefore A.
short_explanation: The recommendation to plant more trees is the conclusion, signalled by "therefore"; the temperature, heatwave and shopper claims are reasons.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Callum says: "The town library should stay open later on weeknights. Many parents cannot bring their children in before it closes at 5 pm because they do not finish work until 5:30 pm. Also, last month's survey showed that borrowing goes up whenever opening hours are extended."

Which one of the following is a reason Callum gives to support his conclusion?
option_a: The town library should stay open later on weeknights.
option_b: Children prefer to read at home in the evenings.
option_c: Many parents finish work after the library has already closed.
option_d: Borrowing went down when the library extended its opening hours.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"This is Callum's conclusion - the claim his reasons support - not one of the reasons.","B":"Callum never says anything about children preferring to read at home.","C":"Correct: Callum states that many parents cannot visit before 5 pm because they finish work at 5:30 pm; this directly supports staying open later.","D":"This is the opposite of what Callum says: the survey showed borrowing goes up when hours are extended."}
worked_solution: First find the conclusion: the library should stay open later on weeknights. A reason is a statement given to support that conclusion. Callum offers two: parents finish work after closing time, and borrowing rises when hours are extended. Option A is the conclusion itself, B is never stated, and D reverses the survey result. The correct answer is therefore C.
short_explanation: Callum supports his conclusion with the fact that many parents finish work after the 5 pm closing time.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Rosa says: "Our school's new recycling bins are working. Since the bins arrived last term, there has been less rubbish lying around the playground. If we put bins on the sports oval as well, the whole school will be tidier."

Which one of these statements, if true, most strengthens Rosa's argument?
option_a: The recycling bins are bright yellow and easy to notice.
option_b: Some students still drop rubbish near the canteen.
option_c: The school bought the bins at a discounted price.
option_d: A count by the environment club found that playground litter halved in the first month after the bins were installed.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"The colour of the bins tells us nothing about whether they have actually reduced rubbish.","B":"This slightly weakens the argument by suggesting the bins have not fixed the problem everywhere.","C":"The price of the bins is irrelevant to whether they are working.","D":"Correct: An actual measured count showing litter halved after the bins arrived gives strong evidence that the bins are working."}
worked_solution: Rosa's argument rests on the claim that the bins have reduced playground rubbish. The statement that helps most is one that adds solid evidence for that claim. A measured litter count showing rubbish halved after the bins were installed does exactly that. A and C are true-but-irrelevant details, and B points the other way. The correct answer is therefore D.
short_explanation: A measured litter count halving after the bins arrived directly supports Rosa's claim that the bins are working.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Dr Okafor says: "The honeyeaters left the Marlo wetlands three weeks earlier this year than they usually do. I believe this is because the insects they feed on hatched earlier than usual."

Which one of these statements, if true, most strengthens Dr Okafor's explanation?
option_a: This year the wetlands' water levels, temperatures and predator numbers were all the same as in previous years.
option_b: Honeyeaters feed mainly on nectar and insects.
option_c: A few honeyeaters stayed at the wetlands all year round.
option_d: Dr Okafor has visited the wetlands every spring for ten years.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Correct: If everything else at the wetlands stayed the same, other possible causes of the early departure are ruled out, leaving the early insect hatching as the most likely explanation.","B":"This is background information the explanation already relies on; it does not make the insect explanation more likely than any other.","C":"A few birds staying behind neither supports nor challenges the explanation for why most left early.","D":"Long experience makes Dr Okafor a careful observer, but it does not show that the insects, rather than something else, caused the early departure."}
worked_solution: Dr Okafor is explaining why the birds left early: the insects hatched early. Something else, like changed water levels or more predators, could also have driven the birds away. Statement A rules those rival explanations out, which makes the insect explanation much stronger. B is mere background, C is beside the point, and D supports the observation rather than the explanation. The correct answer is therefore A.
short_explanation: Showing that water, temperature and predators were unchanged rules out rival causes, leaving the early insect hatching as the best explanation.
license_notes: Original AI-generated content modelled on the style of NSW Selective practice papers; no text copied or paraphrased from any published material.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Ms Ferreira argues: "Students who use screens less in the evening fall asleep earlier and sleep more soundly. Our school should therefore ask families to switch off screens by 8 pm on school nights, because this will help students arrive at school more alert."

Which one of these statements, if true, most strengthens Ms Ferreira's argument?
option_a: Most students enjoy using screens in the evening.
option_b: A large study has found that students who fall asleep earlier and sleep more soundly are noticeably more alert at school the next day.
option_c: Screen devices are cheaper to buy now than they were five years ago.
option_d: Some families already switch off their screens by 8 pm.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Students enjoying screens does not connect better sleep to alertness; if anything it hints the plan may be unpopular.","B":"Correct: Ms Ferreira quietly assumes that better sleep leads to more alertness at school; this study confirms that missing link.","C":"The price of devices has nothing to do with sleep or alertness.","D":"A few families already doing this tells us nothing about whether the plan will make students more alert."}
worked_solution: Ms Ferreira's evidence is about sleep, but her conclusion is about alertness at school. Her argument silently assumes that earlier, sounder sleep actually makes students more alert the next day. Statement B confirms exactly that hidden link, so it strengthens the argument most. A, C and D do not connect sleep to alertness at all. The correct answer is therefore B.
short_explanation: The argument jumps from better sleep to more alertness; the study confirms that hidden link between the two.
license_notes: Original AI-generated content modelled on the style of NSW Selective practice papers; no text copied or paraphrased from any published material.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Marco writes: "A recent report found that tap water in Kurrajong Heights is just as safe to drink as bottled water. The school canteen should therefore stop selling bottled water. Some parents, however, say the report cannot be trusted."

Which one of these statements, if true, most strengthens Marco's argument?
option_a: Bottled water costs much more than tap water.
option_b: The report was easy to find on the council's website.
option_c: The report was produced by an independent laboratory that tested the town's tap water every week for a year and has no links to any water company.
option_d: Many students say they prefer the taste of bottled water.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Price gives a different reason to avoid bottled water, but it does not answer the parents' doubt about the safety report Marco relies on.","B":"Where the report was published says nothing about whether its findings can be trusted.","C":"Correct: An independent laboratory with no ties to any water company, testing weekly for a year, makes the report far more reliable, which directly answers the parents' objection.","D":"Students preferring the taste of bottled water works against Marco's proposal rather than supporting it."}
worked_solution: Marco's whole argument depends on the report being trustworthy, and the parents attack exactly that point. The statement that helps most is one that shows the report is reliable. C does this: the testing was thorough, long-running and carried out by an independent laboratory with no reason to favour either side. A offers a different argument rather than defending this one, B is irrelevant, and D counts against Marco. The correct answer is therefore C.
short_explanation: Showing the report came from a thorough, independent laboratory answers the doubt about its trustworthiness, which Marco's argument depends on.
license_notes: Original AI-generated content modelled on the style of NSW Selective practice papers; no text copied or paraphrased from any published material.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Glen says: "Every stall at the Tallowbark twilight markets sells handmade goods. So whatever you buy at the markets is sure to be handmade."

Which one of these statements, if true, most weakens Glen's argument?
option_a: Handmade goods are usually more expensive than factory-made goods.
option_b: The twilight markets are only held twice a month.
option_c: Most shoppers go to the markets especially to find handmade goods.
option_d: One stall at the markets sells factory-made phone cases alongside its handmade candles.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"The price of handmade goods has no bearing on whether everything sold at the markets is handmade.","B":"How often the markets are held is irrelevant to what the stalls sell.","C":"What shoppers are looking for does not tell us what is actually for sale.","D":"Correct: This is a direct counterexample - a stall can sell some handmade goods yet also sell factory-made items, so a purchase is not guaranteed to be handmade."}
worked_solution: Glen slides from "every stall sells handmade goods" to "everything sold is handmade". A stall that sells some handmade goods might also sell factory-made ones. Statement D describes exactly such a stall, giving a counterexample that breaks Glen's conclusion: you could buy a factory-made phone case at the markets. A, B and C are all irrelevant to what is actually sold. The correct answer is therefore D.
short_explanation: A stall selling factory-made phone cases is a counterexample: "every stall sells handmade goods" does not mean everything sold is handmade.
license_notes: Original AI-generated content modelled on the style of NSW Selective practice papers; no text copied or paraphrased from any published material.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Harriet says: "Our class did ten minutes of stretching before this week's spelling test, and our average score was higher than last week's. The stretching must be what improved our results."

Which one of these statements, if true, most weakens Harriet's argument?
option_a: This week's spelling list was made up of words the class had already practised earlier in the year.
option_b: Stretching also helps to prevent muscle injuries.
option_c: The class did the stretching in the hall rather than in the classroom.
option_d: Most students said they enjoyed the stretching.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Correct: If the words were already familiar, that easily explains the higher scores without the stretching, so Harriet can no longer claim stretching \"must\" be the cause.","B":"Muscle injuries have nothing to do with spelling scores.","C":"Where the stretching happened does not affect whether it caused the improvement.","D":"Enjoying the stretching neither supports nor challenges the claim that it improved the scores."}
worked_solution: Harriet assumes the stretching caused the better scores because it happened first. Her argument collapses if something else could explain the improvement just as well. A familiar spelling list is exactly such an alternative explanation: the class may simply have found this week's words easier. B, C and D leave her causal claim untouched. The correct answer is therefore A.
short_explanation: A familiar spelling list offers a rival explanation for the higher scores, so stretching need not be the cause.
license_notes: Original AI-generated content modelled on the style of NSW Selective practice papers; no text copied or paraphrased from any published material.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Aisha says: "The council is planting five hundred new trees along Beacon Street this winter. By next summer, Beacon Street's footpaths will be much cooler places to walk."

Which one of these statements, if true, most weakens Aisha's argument?
option_a: Some residents asked the council for flower beds instead of trees.
option_b: The saplings being planted take at least fifteen years to grow large enough to shade a footpath.
option_c: Beacon Street is currently the hottest street in the town centre.
option_d: The council has successfully planted trees in other streets before.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"What some residents wanted instead has no effect on whether the trees will cool the street.","B":"Correct: Aisha assumes the new trees will provide shade within a year; if the saplings need fifteen years to cast useful shade, her conclusion about next summer fails.","C":"How hot the street is now explains why the trees are wanted, but it does not challenge Aisha's prediction.","D":"Past successful plantings, if anything, support the council's plan rather than weakening Aisha's claim."}
worked_solution: Aisha's conclusion is about next summer, but her evidence is only that trees are being planted this winter. She silently assumes that newly planted trees will provide cooling shade almost immediately. Statement B destroys that hidden assumption: saplings needing fifteen years to shade a footpath cannot cool the street by next summer. A and C are irrelevant, and D mildly supports the plan. The correct answer is therefore B.
short_explanation: Aisha assumes new trees give shade quickly; saplings needing fifteen years to shade a footpath undermine her "by next summer" claim.
license_notes: Original AI-generated content modelled on the style of NSW Selective practice papers; no text copied or paraphrased from any published material.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Deng says: "I surveyed twenty students at our school's after-school coding club, and sixteen of them said extra screen time helps them learn. Clearly, most students at our school believe extra screen time helps learning."

Which one of these statements, if true, most weakens Deng's argument?
option_a: Four of the students Deng surveyed said extra screen time does not help them learn.
option_b: The survey was carried out on a Friday afternoon.
option_c: Students who join the coding club are much keener on computers than most students at the school.
option_d: Several teachers at the school believe screen time should be limited.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Deng's figures already include these four students, so this adds nothing new.","B":"The day of the survey does not affect who was asked or what they believe.","C":"Correct: If coding club members are unusually keen on computers, they are not representative of the whole school, so Deng cannot generalise from them to \"most students\".","D":"Teachers' opinions are about a different group and a different question; Deng's claim concerns what students believe."}
worked_solution: Deng generalises from twenty coding club members to the whole school. That only works if the club members think like typical students. Statement C shows they probably do not: people who choose a coding club are likely to be far more positive about screens than average. This makes the sample unrepresentative and the generalisation unsafe. A is already counted in the data, and B and D do not touch the sample problem. The correct answer is therefore C.
short_explanation: Coding club members are unusually keen on computers, so the sample cannot represent the views of the whole school.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Ingrid says: "Tam trains with the Riverbend running club every week, so Tam is certain to finish the five-kilometre fun run in under thirty minutes."

Which one of the following is an assumption Ingrid has made?
option_a: Tam has entered a fun run at least once before.
option_b: People who do not train with a running club cannot finish the fun run in under thirty minutes.
option_c: Tam trains harder than anyone else at the running club.
option_d: Everyone who trains weekly with the running club can finish the fun run in under thirty minutes.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Ingrid's reasoning does not need Tam to have entered a fun run before; it relies only on Tam's weekly training.","B":"This is about people who do not train, which Ingrid says nothing about; her claim concerns what training guarantees, not what its absence prevents.","C":"Ingrid does not need Tam to be the hardest trainer, only for weekly training to be enough.","D":"Correct: To get from \"Tam trains weekly with the club\" to \"Tam is certain to finish in under thirty minutes\", Ingrid must assume that weekly club training guarantees such a time."}
worked_solution: Ingrid's evidence is that Tam trains weekly; her conclusion is that Tam is certain to run under thirty minutes. The argument only works if there is a link joining the two: weekly club training must guarantee a sub-thirty-minute run. That unstated link is D. Option B is the inverse claim about non-trainers, which Ingrid never relies on, and A and C are details her argument does not need. The correct answer is therefore D.
short_explanation: The argument needs the missing link that weekly club training guarantees finishing in under thirty minutes.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Priya says: "Our street should hold a monthly market to raise money for new playground equipment. The stall fees from just twenty stalls each month would cover the cost of the equipment within a year."

Which one of the following is an assumption Priya has made?
option_a: At least twenty stallholders would be willing to pay the stall fee each month.
option_b: New playground equipment is more expensive than second-hand equipment.
option_c: Street markets are already held in many other towns.
option_d: The playground is used by many children in the street.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Correct: Priya's plan only raises the money if twenty fee-paying stallholders actually turn up every month; she assumes this is achievable without stating it.","B":"Comparing new and second-hand prices plays no part in her reasoning.","C":"Other towns' markets are not needed for her calculation to work.","D":"How many children use the playground might explain why the equipment is wanted, but the fundraising plan does not depend on it."}
worked_solution: Priya's sums are based on fees from twenty stalls a month, but she never shows that twenty stallholders would actually come and pay. If fewer turned up, the plan would fail, so her argument silently assumes the market can really attract that many. B, C and D are details her calculation does not rely on. The correct answer is therefore A.
short_explanation: The plan only works if twenty fee-paying stallholders actually attend each month - an unstated feasibility assumption.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Callum says: "Since the council installed free water refill stations at the swimming pool last summer, sales of bottled water at the pool kiosk have fallen. The refill stations must be what caused the drop in sales."

Which one of the following is an assumption Callum has made?
option_a: Refill stations are cheaper for the council to run than a kiosk.
option_b: Nothing else changed at the pool that could have reduced bottled water sales.
option_c: Everyone who uses a refill station used to buy bottled water at the kiosk.
option_d: The kiosk will soon stop selling bottled water altogether.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Running costs are irrelevant to Callum's claim about what caused the drop in sales.","B":"Correct: Callum concludes the stations \"must\" be the cause, which is only safe if no other change - fewer visitors, higher prices, cooler weather - could explain the fall in sales.","C":"This is far stronger than Callum needs; sales could fall even if only some refill users used to buy bottled water.","D":"Callum's argument is about what has already caused the drop, not about what the kiosk will do in future."}
worked_solution: Callum notices that the sales drop followed the refill stations and concludes the stations must be the cause. That conclusion is only certain if every other possible cause is off the table: if visitor numbers, prices or the weather had also changed, they could explain the drop instead. So Callum is assuming no such rival cause exists, which is B. C overstates what he needs, and A and D are beside the point. The correct answer is therefore B.
short_explanation: Concluding the stations "must" be the cause assumes nothing else at the pool could have reduced bottled water sales.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
To hold a stall at the Riverbend twilight market, a stallholder must have public liability insurance. In addition, any stallholder who sells food must also hold a food-safety certificate.

Rosa says: "Marco holds a food-safety certificate, so he must be selling food at the market."

Glen says: "Ingrid holds a stall at the market, so she must have public liability insurance."

Whose reasoning is correct?
option_a: Rosa only
option_b: Glen only
option_c: Both Rosa and Glen
option_d: Neither Rosa nor Glen
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Rosa's reasoning is flawed: the rule says food sellers need a certificate, not that everyone with a certificate sells food. Marco may simply hold one without selling food.","B":"Correct: Glen applies the rule properly - every stallholder must have public liability insurance, and Ingrid is a stallholder, so she must have it. Rosa reverses her rule.","C":"Only Glen reasons correctly; Rosa commits a reversal error.","D":"Glen's conclusion does follow from the rules, so it is not true that neither is correct."}
worked_solution: The rules say: all stallholders need insurance, and food sellers additionally need a food-safety certificate. Glen argues from "Ingrid holds a stall" to "Ingrid has insurance", which follows directly from the first rule. Rosa argues in reverse: the rule says selling food requires a certificate, not that holding a certificate means you sell food - Marco could hold one for any reason. Rosa's reasoning fails while Glen's holds. The correct answer is therefore B.
short_explanation: Glen applies the insurance rule correctly; Rosa reverses the food rule - holding a certificate does not prove Marco sells food.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Every honeyeater that hatched at the Marlo wetlands this spring was fitted with a silver leg ring by Dr Okafor's research team. Some visiting honeyeaters from other wetlands also wear silver leg rings fitted by other research teams.

Harriet says: "That honeyeater has no leg ring at all, so it cannot have hatched at the Marlo wetlands this spring."

Tam says: "That honeyeater has a silver leg ring, so it must have hatched at the Marlo wetlands this spring."

Whose reasoning is correct?
option_a: Harriet only
option_b: Tam only
option_c: Both Harriet and Tam
option_d: Neither Harriet nor Tam
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Correct: Harriet reasons validly - every bird hatched there this spring has a ring, so a bird with no ring cannot have hatched there. Tam ignores the visiting ringed birds.","B":"Tam's reasoning fails: visiting honeyeaters from other wetlands also wear silver rings, so a ringed bird need not have hatched at Marlo.","C":"Only Harriet is correct; Tam overlooks that visiting birds may also wear silver rings.","D":"Harriet's conclusion follows correctly from the information, so it is not true that neither is correct."}
worked_solution: The information says all this spring's Marlo hatchlings have silver rings, but some ringed birds are visitors from elsewhere. Harriet uses the rule the right way round: no ring means the bird cannot be one of this spring's hatchlings, since all of those are ringed. Tam runs the rule backwards: a silver ring does not prove a Marlo hatching, because visitors also wear silver rings. So Harriet reasons correctly and Tam does not. The correct answer is therefore A.
short_explanation: No ring rules out a Marlo hatchling (all were ringed), but a ring proves nothing because visiting birds wear rings too.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Deng says: "Everyone who stayed up late gaming last night is tired at school today. Aisha is tired today, so she must have stayed up late gaming."

Which one of the following sentences shows the mistake Deng has made?
option_a: Just because Aisha is tired today, it does not mean that she will be tired tomorrow.
option_b: Just because someone stayed up late, it does not mean that they were gaming.
option_c: Just because everyone who stayed up late gaming is tired, it does not mean that gaming is the only possible reason for being tired.
option_d: Just because gaming can make people tired, it does not mean that everyone who games feels tired.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Deng says nothing about tomorrow; his mistake is about what being tired today proves.","B":"Deng's starting statement is about people who stayed up late gaming, so separating late nights from gaming is not the error he makes.","C":"Correct: Deng treats tiredness as proof of gaming, but tiredness can have many causes - the rule only runs from gaming to tiredness, not back again.","D":"Deng's given statement already says everyone who gamed late is tired; his error is not about whether gaming always causes tiredness."}
worked_solution: Deng's rule runs one way: late-night gaming leads to tiredness. He then reasons backwards, treating Aisha's tiredness as proof that she gamed late. But she could be tired for any number of reasons - a sports carnival, a late bus, a poor night's sleep. The mistake is assuming gaming is the only path to tiredness, which is what C says. The correct answer is therefore C.
short_explanation: Deng reasons backwards: being tired does not prove gaming, because gaming is not the only possible cause of tiredness.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Ms Ferreira says: "No stallholder who sold out early stayed until closing time at the harvest market. Rosa stayed until closing time, so Rosa cannot have sold anything at all."

Which one of the following sentences shows the mistake Ms Ferreira has made?
option_a: Just because Rosa stayed until closing time, it does not mean that she enjoyed the market.
option_b: Just because no stallholder who sold out early stayed until closing, it does not mean that the market closed late.
option_c: Just because a stallholder sold out early, it does not mean that they went straight home.
option_d: Just because Rosa did not sell out early, it does not mean that she sold nothing at all.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Whether Rosa enjoyed the market is beside the point; the flaw concerns what her staying late proves about her sales.","B":"The closing time of the market is not part of Ms Ferreira's reasoning.","C":"What early sellers did after selling out is irrelevant to the conclusion about Rosa.","D":"Correct: From the facts we can only conclude that Rosa did not sell out early; jumping from that to \"she sold nothing at all\" is the overreach in the argument."}
worked_solution: From the given rule, anyone who sold out early left before closing, so Rosa, who stayed to closing, cannot have sold out early - that much is valid. Ms Ferreira then leaps much further, concluding Rosa sold nothing at all. But not selling out early is quite different from selling nothing: Rosa may have sold half her goods. D names exactly that overreach. The correct answer is therefore D.
short_explanation: Staying late only shows Rosa did not sell out early; it is an overreach to conclude she sold nothing at all.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
The Kurrajong Gazette claims that the new footbridge over Sandy Creek has reduced car traffic on Miller Road.

Which one of the following pieces of evidence, if true, would best support the newspaper's claim?
option_a: Council traffic counters recorded thirty per cent fewer cars on Miller Road in the three months after the footbridge opened than in the three months before.
option_b: A shop owner on Miller Road says the street feels quieter than it used to.
option_c: The footbridge cost less to build than the council expected.
option_d: The number of people walking across the footbridge grows every week.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Correct: Measured counts comparing traffic before and after the bridge opened give direct, objective evidence that car traffic on Miller Road has fallen.","B":"One person's impression that the street \"feels quieter\" is a weak anecdote, not a measurement of traffic.","C":"The cost of building the bridge says nothing about traffic on Miller Road.","D":"More people walking across the bridge does not show that fewer cars are using Miller Road - the walkers might never have driven."}
worked_solution: The claim is about car traffic on Miller Road going down, so the best evidence measures exactly that. Option A does: official counters, a clear before-and-after comparison, and a large measured drop. B is a single impression, C is about money, and D measures walkers rather than cars - popularity of the bridge does not prove fewer car trips. The correct answer is therefore A.
short_explanation: Official before-and-after traffic counts directly measure the claimed drop in cars; the other options are anecdote or beside the point.

question_text: At Zainab's market stall, prices are written in a secret code where each letter always stands for the same digit. She has written FIG as 4 2 7 and FUN as 4 9 5. Using the same code, what number does the word GUN stand for?
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Correct: from FIG, G = 7; from FUN, U = 9 and N = 5; so GUN = 7, 9, 5 = 795.","B":"725 keeps G = 7 but wrongly makes U = 2 from the word FIG.","C":"975 reverses the digits, putting N before G.","D":"792 swaps N for the digit 2 taken from the wrong word."}
worked_solution: Match letters to digits from the two coded words. FIG = 4 2 7 means F = 4, I = 2, G = 7. FUN = 4 9 5 means F = 4, U = 9, N = 5. So GUN = G, U, N = 7, 9, 5 = 795. The correct answer is therefore A.
